column2: Sickle Cell Disease & Healthcare System Navigation – Qualitative Study

column6: sub_theme_description
column7: example_paraphrased

column3: Experiences related to loss of coverage gaps in insurance and the burden of navigating prior authorizations and denials
column6: Periods of being uninsured or underinsured due to job loss, Medicaid redetermination, or aging off a parent's plan
column7: Described losing Medicaid coverage unexpectedly after a redetermination and being unable to afford pain medication out of pocket for two months

column6: Time and energy required to obtain prior authorizations for established treatments including hydroxyurea and opioid pain management
column7: Described spending hours each week on phone calls with insurance to obtain approval for a medication they had been taking for five years

column6: Experiences of outright denial of medically necessary care and the process of filing appeals
column7: Had a hospitalization claim denied and spent three months appealing with documentation from their hematologist before it was approved

column3: Impact of losing established providers and the emotional and clinical challenges of rebuilding therapeutic relationships
column6: Distress caused by loss of a known and trusted specialist due to provider turnover or insurance network changes
column7: Described their long-term hematologist leaving the practice as one of the most destabilizing events in their care history

column6: Difficulty establishing care with a new provider who lacks knowledge of patient's history and treatment preferences
column7: Expressed frustration at having to 're-prove' their pain management needs to a new provider who was skeptical of their reported pain levels

column5: Transition from Pediatric to Adult Care
column6: Challenges navigating the shift from pediatric to adult SCD care systems as young adults
column7: Described feeling 'dropped' when transitioning out of the pediatric system with no clear handoff or introduction to adult care providers

column3: Reliance on the emergency department as a default care setting when specialist access is unavailable
column5: ED as Default for Pain Crises
column6: Patterns of presenting to the ED for pain crises due to lack of after-hours specialist access or infusion center availability
column7: Described going to the ED at least once a month because their hematology clinic had no after-hours or weekend availability

column6: Experiences of stigma, dismissal, or inadequate pain management in ED settings
column7: Reported being told by an ED nurse that their pain rating seemed 'too high' and feeling their SCD was not taken seriously

column6: Expressed desire to avoid ED but felt it was the only option available for acute crises
column7: Described dreading ED visits due to long waits and stigma but feeling there was no alternative during severe pain crises

column3: Barriers and delays in obtaining referrals to hematology and other specialty services
column6: Extended wait times between primary care referral and specialist appointment
column7: Waited four months for a new hematology appointment after insurance forced a provider change

column6: Insurance network restrictions preventing access to preferred or previously established specialists
column7: Was told their preferred hematologist was out-of-network under the new insurance plan and would have to start over with an in-network provider

column6: Active strategies used to expedite or secure referrals
column7: Described calling the insurance company daily and asking their primary care doctor to mark the referral as urgent to speed up the process

column3: Psychological and emotional toll of repeatedly engaging with a complex and often adversarial healthcare system
column6: Feelings of powerlessness in the face of system barriers
column7: Described feeling like 'fighting a war every month' just to get basic care approved

column6: Fatigue associated with the sustained effort required to navigate the system
column7: Expressed that the mental energy spent on insurance and referrals left them with less capacity to manage their actual disease

column6: Anxiety and depression associated with healthcare access uncertainty
column7: Described increased anxiety around the time of annual insurance renewals due to fear of losing coverage or needing to change providers

column3: Active strategies used by participants to navigate barriers and maintain access to care
column6: Maintaining personal health records and documentation to support care navigation
column7: Kept a personal binder of all medical records, lab results, and prior authorization letters to bring to every appointment and insurance call

column6: Relying on family members or patient advocates to assist with system navigation
column7: Described their mother handling most insurance calls because they did not have the emotional capacity to do so during pain crises

column6: Learning system navigation strategies from other SCD patients in community groups
column7: Learned about a hospital-based SCD care coordinator program from a Facebook support group

column3: Patient-generated suggestions for policy and practice changes to improve SCD care
column6: Desire for a single point of contact to manage care coordination across providers
column7: Said a dedicated care coordinator who knew their full history would eliminate most of the stress they currently experienced

column6: Calls for blanket prior authorization or exemption for established SCD treatments
column7: Suggested that medications a patient has been on for more than one year should be automatically approved without annual reauthorization

column6: Recommendations for structured handoffs between pediatric and adult care and between providers
column7: Recommended a formal transition program with at least two joint appointments between pediatric and adult providers before full transfer
